content.full_text:
STATE OF CALIFORNIA 
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION 
• 428 J Street • Suite 620 • Sacramento, CA 95814-2329 
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886 
October 14, 2015 
Alan Seem 
Legal Counsel 
Shearman & Sterling, LLP 
1460 El Camino Real, 2nd Floor 
Menlo Park, CA 94025-41 I 0 
Re: 
Your Request for Informal Assistance 
Our File No. 1-15-189 
Dear Mr. Seem: 
This letter responds to your request for advice regarding the gift provisions of the Political 
Reform Act (the "Act") 1 on behalf of the China Silicon Valley Business Development ("CSV"), a 
California non-profit corporation, and several unnamed mayors. 
Note that the Act imposes duties on public officials and on donors to the officials.2 Because 
your questions are general in nature and you have not named officials on whose behalf you request 
advice, we are treating your request as one for informal assistance. Informal assistance does not 
provide the requestor with the immunity provided by an opinion or fonnal written advice. (Section 
83114; Regulation 18329( c)(3 ).) Finally, because the Fair Political Practices Commission (the 
"Commission") does not act as a finder of fact when rendering advice (In re Oglesby (1975) 1 
FPPC Ops. 71 ), this letter is based on the facts presented. 
FACTS 
You are writing on behalf of the China Silicon Valley September 2015 Mayors' China Trip 
(the "2015 China Trip") organized by CSV. You stated that CSV is organizing a trip for Silicon 
Valley mayors to travel to China as part of a 2015 China Trip. You stated that the purpose, 
sponsorship, and certain arrangements of the 2015 China Trip are similar to the Silicon Valley 
Mayors' China Trip CSV organized in June 2014 (the "2014 China Trip"). 
The 2015 China Trip is aimed at improving business cooperation between China and the 
Silicon Valley and helping create jobs and increase economic activity in both China and the Silicon 
1 The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory 
references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices 
Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All 
regulatory references are to Title 2. Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated. 
2 Section 89521 provides: "Any person who makes or receives an honorarium, gift, or expenditure in violation 
of this chapter is liable in a civil action brought by the Commission for an amount of up to three times the amount of the 
unlawful honorarium, gift, or expenditure. 


File No. I-15-189 
Page No. 2 
Valley. The principal purpose of the 2015 China Trip is to facilitate investment and international 
trade, and promote communications between China and the Silicon Valley region. While in China, 
the mayors will have opportunities to participate in meetings with local government officials, 
potential investors and CEOs From local high tech companies. The proposed itinerary provides: 
"The CSV 2015 Mayors China Trip will depart from San Francisco on Sept. 19,!3! 
visit Yancheng City (Jiangsu Province), Shanghai Municipality, and Chengdu 
City (Sichuan Province), and return from Chengdu to San Francisco on Sept. 27-
26. The itinerary is as follows: 
"Sept. 20 
Evening: Arrive Yancheng 
··sept. 21 
Morning: Visit high-tech park and companies in Yancheng 
Afternoon: Meet with officials and business leaders ofYancheng 
"Sept. 22 
Morning: Visit high-tech park 
Afternoon: Flight from Yancheng to Shanghai 
"Sept. 23 
Morning: Flight from Shanghai to Chengdu 
Afternoon: Meet with officials of Sichuan Province 
"Sept. 24 
Morning: Opening ceremony of the Western China Overseas High Tech and High 
Talents Conference (OHTC) 
Afternoon: Collaboration dialogue between Mayors from Sichuan Province and 
Mayors from Silicon Valley 
"Sept. 25 
Morning: Visit Chengdu 
Afternoon: Visit high-tech park and companies in Mianyang 
"Sept. 26 
Morning: Visit Shuangliu, Chengdu 
Afternoon: Conference with companies in Chengdu 
"Sept. 27 
Morning: Visit companies in Chengdu 
3 As we discussed, nonnally the Commission will not advise on conduct that has already occurred. Because 
your request for advice was received virtually on the same day that the: officials were to receive free travel, this could be 
considered past conduct However, as we discussed, if no exception applied, recipients will have 30 days to reimburse 
the donor in order to avoid violating the gift limit of the Act. Thus, we provide this advice prospectively in light of the 
return rule in Section 82028. 


Afternoon: Departure for SF (indirect flight) 
"Sept. 28 
Morning: Departure for SF (direct flight) 
"The itinerary of the optional 3-day trip to Guangzhou City (Guangdong 
Province) 
"Sept. 27 
Morning: Visit companies in Chengdu 
Afternoon: Flight from Chengdu to Guangzhou 
"Sept. 26 
Morning: Visit high-tech park and companies in Guangzhou Panyu District 
Afternoon: Meet with officials and business leaders of Panyu District 
"Sept. 29 
Morning: Visit companies in Guangzhou 
Afternoon: Visit companies in Guangzhou 
"Sept. 30 
Morning: Departure for SF 
With respect to financing, you stated: 
File No. 1-15-189 
Page No. 3 
• 
All international air travel expenses to and from China, approximately $2,000 per 
person, and the remaining expenses for travel, lodging and meals within China during 
the 2015 China Trip, approximately $3,000 per person, will be provided by local 
Chinese government authorities, including the Foreign Affairs Office of Sichuan 
Province, the Foreign Affairs Office of Yancheng City and the Foreign Affairs Office of 
Guangzhou Panyu District. 
• 
CSV will not provide any funds or services with monetary value to the mayors in 
connection with the 2015 China Trip. 
ANALYSIS 
J. Are tl,e payme11ts subject to tl,e Gift Limits oft/1e Act? 
A gift is defined under Section 82028(a) as "any payment that confers a personal benefit on 
the recipient, to the extent that consideration of equal or greater value is not received and includes a 
rebate or discount in the price of anything of value unless the rebate or discount is made in the 
regular course of business to members of the public without regard to official status." In an effort to 
reduce improper influences on public officials, the Act regulates the receipt of gifts by local public 
officials in three ways: 


File No. 1-15-189 
Page No. 4 
• 
First, the Act places limitations on the acceptance of gifts by certain public officials. The 
current limit is $460 from a single source in a calendar year. (Section 89503; Regulation 
18940.2.) This gift limit applies to all Mayors, among other officials. (Sections 89503(a) & 
87200.) 
• 
Second, so that the public is made aware of any potential influences from gifts, the Act 
imposes reporting obligations on certain public officials, including Mayors, requiring that 
any gift (or any gifts that aggregate to $50 or more from the same source} received during 
the calendar year are disclosed on the officials' statements of economic interests. (Sections 
87200 - 87210.) 
• Third, the Act prohibits any public official from making, participating in making, or using 
his or her position to influence the outcome of a governmental decision involving the donor 
of a gift or gifts with an aggregate value of $460 or more provided to, received by, or 
promised to the official within the 12 months prior to the date the decision is made. 
(Sections 87100 & 87103(e); Regulations 18700 & 18703.4.) 
Under the Act, any travel is presumed to confer some personal benefit on a public official. 
In addition, travel costs paid for by a third party generally are reportable gifts under the Act. 
Therefore, absent an exception, the value of all travel-related expenses to China will be considered 
a reportable gift to each of the Mayors. (Section 82028; Gault Advice Letter, No. A-07-158.) 
2. Exceptions 
Under some circumstances, even though a payment for transportation, lodging, and 
subsistence may be a reportable gift under the Act, the gift is not subject to the Act's current $460 
gift limit. In this regard, Section 89506(a) provides (in pertinent part) an exception from the gift 
limits for certain travel payments: 
"Payments, advances, or reimbursements, for travel, including actual 
transportation and related lodging and subsistence that is reasonably related to a 
legislative or governmental purpose, or to an issue of state, national, or 
international public policy, are not prohibited or limited by this chapter if either of 
the following apply: 
"(1 ) .... 
"(2) The travel is provided by a government, a governmental agency, a 
foreign government, a governmental authority, a bona fide public or private 
educational institution, as defined in Section 203 of the Revenue and Taxation 
Code, a nonprofit organization that is exempt from taxation under Section 
50l (c)(3) of the Internal Revenue Code, or by a person domiciled outside the 
United States which substantially satisfies the requirements for tax - exempt status 
under Section 501(c)(3) of the Internal Revenue Code." 


File No. 1-15-189 
Page No. 5 
A. Legislatfre or gol'ernmenlal purpose, or an issue of stale, national, or internalional 
public policy. 
According to the facts, the trip's purpose is to improve business cooperation between China 
and the Silicon Valley, to help create jobs and increase economic activity in both China and the 
Silicon Valley, to facilitate investment and international trade, and to promote communications 
between China and the Silicon Valley region. While in China, the mayors will have opportunities to 
participate in meetings with local government officials, potential investors, and CEOs from local 
high-tech companies. Based on these facts, the travel is directly related to a legislative or 
governmental purpose. 
B. The travel is provided by a government, a governmental agency, a foreign govemment, a 
govemmental alllhority. 
Additionally, all international air travel expenses to and from China, approximately $2,000 
per person, and the remaining expenses for travel, lodging and meals within China during the 20 t 5 
China Trip, approximately $3,000 per person, wilt be provided by local Chinese government 
authorities, including the Foreign Affairs Office of Sichuan Province, the Foreign Affairs Office of 
Yancheng City, and the Foreign Affairs Office of Guangzhou Panyu District. Therefore, the sources 
of funding for the travel, the foreign city governments, fall within the purview of Section 
89506( a)(2 ). 
Consequently, the specified payments are reportable gifts that are not subject to gift limits 
pursuant to Section 89506(a)(2). Please note, however, that any payments made for items other than 
travel and related lodging and subsistence may be considered a reportable gift subject to the $460 
gift limit and the conflict of interest provisions of the Act because those items would not be related 
to a legislative or governmental purpose. 
Co11jlict of /11terest Disq11alijicatio11 
A public official who receives gift(s) of $460 or more may have a financial conflict of 
interest under the Act. (Sections 87100 et seq.} The official must disqualify himself or herself from 
voting or otherwise participating in a governmental decision affecting that source, if the payment 
was received or promised to the official within 12 months preceding the decision. Therefore, if the 
Mayors accept gifts from the sources identified above valued at $460 or more, they may be 
prohibited from participating in governmental decisions affecting those sources. 


File No. 1-15-189 
Page No. 6 
If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660. 
JWW:jgl 
Sincerely, 
Hyla P. Wagner 
General Counsel 
John W. Wallace 
Assi ant General Counsel, 
Leg Division

sections.conclusion: The specified payments are reportable gifts that are not subject to gift limits pursuant to Section 89506(a)(2), as the travel is provided by foreign government authorities and is directly related to a governmental purpose. However, any payments for items other than travel and related lodging and subsistence may be considered a reportable gift subject to the $460 gift limit, and officials may face conflict of interest disqualification if gifts aggregate to $460 or more.

sections.question_synthetic: Whether payments for a Silicon Valley mayors' trip to China organized by China Silicon Valley Business Development and funded by Chinese government authorities are subject to California Political Reform Act gift limits and conflict of interest rules.
sections.conclusion_synthetic: Travel payments funded by Chinese government authorities for a mayors' trip to facilitate business cooperation and investment qualify for an exception from the Act's $460 gift limit under Section 89506(a)(2). However, the mayors must disclose these gifts and may face conflict of interest disqualification from decisions affecting the funding sources within 12 months.
sections.parsing_notes: Format: modern; LLM: Document includes explicit QUESTION and CONCLUSION sections (reformatted as analysis sections). Marked as informal advice letter providing immunity-free guidance. Includes detailed facts section with trip itinerary and financing information.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: Whether payments for a Silicon Valley mayors' trip to China organized by China Silicon Valley Business Development and funded by Chinese government authorities are subject to California Political Reform Act gift limits and conflict of interest rules.

CONCLUSION: The specified payments are reportable gifts that are not subject to gift limits pursuant to Section 89506(a)(2), as the travel is provided by foreign government authorities and is directly related to a governmental purpose. However, any payments for items other than travel and related lodging and subsistence may be considered a reportable gift subject to the $460 gift limit, and officials may face conflict of interest disqualification if gifts aggregate to $460 or more.

embedding.summary: FPPC provides informal advice regarding gift and conflict of interest provisions of the Political Reform Act for a Silicon Valley mayors' trip to China funded by Chinese government authorities. The advice concludes that the travel payments qualify for an exception from gift limits under Section 89506(a)(2) but remain reportable gifts subject to potential conflict of interest disqualification.
